Review the emitted plan for plan-flow goal **G13** (milestone M50, `goals` ledger). You are ONE of several CONFIGURED reviewers: run in **CONFIGURED MODE** — judge the plan against the canonical `/cq:plan-review` rubric (commands/cq/plan-review.md: fine-grained / sequenced / testable / grounded / complete), then RETURN your verdict as a single fenced ```json block and write NOTHING to any ledger (the orchestrator writes the one aggregated reviews item).

The plan to review:
- Goal G13 "Operability & safety for weighted aggregation + pacing" (read its full description + grounding from the ledger). Binding scope decisions are the ANSWERED questions Q51–Q55 (read them from the questions ledger).
- Work milestones: M51 (E2E overload+sampling harness), M52 (Weighted capacity-sanity guard), M53 (Aggregation-engagement observability), M54 (Probe protection under pacer overload), M55 (Operability documentation).
- Tasks T141–T148 (read each from the `tasks` ledger, with dependsOn edges):
  - T141 (M51): extend netns e2e fixture — sustained-load driver + metrics/log sampling helpers. root.
  - T142 (M52): hard-fail config load when declared link_bandwidth proves weighted aggregation can never engage. root.
  - T143 (M53): expose WeightedScheduler aggregation-gate snapshot + engage/disengage transition log. deps T141.
  - T144 (M52): startup WARN + wanbond_weighted_capacity_sane gauge when capacity unverifiable. deps T142.
  - T145 (M54): reserve probe headroom in weighted pacer — exempt-but-charged probe accounting (reproduce-first). deps T141.
  - T146 (M53): plumb per-peer aggregation gauges through Bind snapshot → metrics.Source → collector. deps T143.
  - T147 (M53): e2e engage/disengage flip + configured-but-inert visibility scenarios. deps T141, T146.
  - T148 (M55): document pacing on/off tradeoff, frame-class priority model, inner-ICMP infeasibility. deps T147, T144, T145.

Judge against the actual repo (internal/sched, internal/config, internal/metrics, internal/bind, internal/telemetry, test/e2e) — verify the plan's grounding claims are real (e.g. that probes bypass scheduler.Pick, that no scheduler→metrics seam exists, that deriveWeightedPacingFromBDP is G2-owned, that the T94 per-peer back-compat rule exists). Check: is each task fine-grained and independently implementable? Are the dependsOn edges correct and complete (no missing/cyclic deps)? Is each acceptance criterion operationally testable (Q55 binds behavioral acceptance to -tags e2e; T148 is a deliberate lint+review-gated docs deviation — judge whether that deviation is justified)? Is the plan complete for the answered scope WITHOUT re-owning G2/Q20's auto-derive+BDP-sizing docs (Q53 Option A boundary)?

Emit EXACTLY the rubric's verdict contract: `{ "summary": "...", "verdict": "go-ahead"|"revise", "new_questions": [...], "criticism": [...], "defects": [...] }`. A `revise` MUST carry non-empty new_questions and/or criticism. End your reply with a `### Session summary` section (goal G13, role: plan-reviewer (fable), the verdict).

- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.
- cq:plan: Start a plan-flow goal — create the goal (from free text OR one-or-more idea-ids, one goal per idea), then hand off to the planner for the first clarifying questions.
- cq:plan-review: Shared adversarial plan-review rubric — judge an emitted plan for a goal against the fine-grained / sequenced / testable / grounded / complete rubric, classify findings into new_questions / criticism / defects, and emit a single fenced-json verdict on stdout. The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.
- cq:plan:advance: Advance plan-flow goals one full round — a given goal, or (no argument) every unlocked goal — running the planner↔reviewer loop until each needs the user or reaches `planned`.
- cq:plan:follow-up: Add new scope to an EXISTING plan-flow goal — append the follow-up request, re-open the goal, and hand to the planner for a fresh clarifying round.
- cq:planners: Session-only planner-set override — parse a natural-language planner instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_planners/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- cq:reviewers: Session-only reviewer-set override — parse a natural-language reviewer instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_reviewers/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

{"items":{"questions":[{"id":"Q51","milestoneId":"M50","status":"answered","fields":{"question":"Item 3(ii) 'latency/priority class so control/probe/ICMP frames aren't dropped under pacer overload': what is the ACTUAL in-scope traffic, given wanbond cannot see inside the encrypted WG tunnel? Confirm the scope split so I can write feasible tasks.","context":"The pacer's only frame-type signal is FrameClass = ClassData | ClassControl (internal/sched/scheduler.go). ClassControl (WG handshake/keepalive) is ALREADY pacing-exempt (D22), which is also what G2 CORE-SCOPE-1 validates ('pacing does not starve WireGuard control frames'). Your empirical '~38% ICMP loss under overload' almost certainly refers to ICMP carried INSIDE the tunnel: to wanbond that is opaque ClassData (encrypted WG transport) indistinguishable from bulk data, so wanbond CANNOT give inner ICMP a priority lane without plaintext DPI before encryption (out of architecture). What IS addressable and wanbond-visible: (a) wanbond's OWN telemetry PROBE frames (frame.KindProbe) being shed by the pacer under overload, which would cause spurious path-DOWN / failover flaps; (b) a generic small-frame / low-rate priority lane for ClassData (heuristic, since inner type is invisible). Which of these does item 3(ii) mean, and is inner-ICMP prioritization explicitly OUT of scope?","suggestions":["Scope item 3(ii) to protecting wanbond-generated PROBE frames from pacer shedding (add a probe/priority class so overload can't induce spurious path-down), and EXCLUDE inner-ICMP prioritization as architecturally infeasible (document why).","Add BOTH a probe-protection class AND a generic small-frame/low-rate ClassData priority lane (best-effort, since inner type is opaque), excluding inner-ICMP as out of scope.","Something else / inner-ICMP really was OUTER host traffic competing on the netns link (then wanbond cannot prioritize it either) - clarify the empirical setup."],"recommendation":"Option 1: protect PROBE frames (wanbond-visible, real operability impact - prevents pacer overload from faking path-down), and explicitly document inner-ICMP prioritization as out of scope because the tunnel payload is opaque to the pacer.","ledgerRefs":["goals:G13"],"answer":"as recommended"},"createdAt":"2026-07-14T12:16:25.034Z","updatedAt":"2026-07-14T12:22:59.940Z","author":"user","session":"915ea040-10d3-4f13-9cf2-ed8e5149babb"},{"id":"Q52","milestoneId":"M50","status":"answered","fields":{"question":"Item 2 capacity-sanity guard: should selecting policy="weighted" with a per_path_capacity_fps the declared link can never approach be a HARD config-load FAILURE (fail-fast, refuse to start) or a startup WARN (log + metric, still runs)? And what is the exact trip criterion?","context":"Today per_path_capacity_fps defaults to a synthetic 10000 (~115 Mbit/s); on a low-throughput/CPU-bound edge, EngageFraction*PerPathCapacity is never reached so aggregation is silently inert (you had to hand-set 250). A precise, testable trip rule is needed. Candidate criterion using the existing SizePacingFromBDP helper: when weighted is selected AND link_bandwidth is declared, compute impliedCapacityFPS = link_bandwidth/(8*avgWireFrameBytes); if the configured EngageFraction*per_path_capacity_fps exceeds impliedCapacityFPS (aggregation mathematically cannot engage at line rate), trip the guard. Open sub-question: if weighted is selected but link_bandwidth is NOT declared, is that itself a WARN/FAIL (no way to sanity-check), or allowed? 'Fail-loud' in your text is ambiguous between a hard fail and a loud warning.","suggestions":["Hard config-load FAILURE (fail-fast per project guidelines): weighted + a capacity the declared link cannot approach refuses to start with an actionable error; weighted + undeclared link_bandwidth also fails (cannot verify).","Startup WARN only (log line + a wanbond_weighted_capacity_sane gauge=0): never blocks startup, surfaces the misconfig for the operator/dashboard to catch.","WARN when link_bandwidth is undeclared, but FAIL when it IS declared and the numbers prove aggregation can never engage."],"recommendation":"Option 3: a declared-bandwidth contradiction is a determinable misconfiguration -> fail fast (matches the repo's fail-fast/validate-at-boundary discipline); an undeclared bandwidth can only be WARNed since there is nothing to check against. This also dovetails with the metric from item 1.","ledgerRefs":["goals:G13"],"answer":"as recommended"},"createdAt":"2026-07-14T12:16:37.613Z","updatedAt":"2026-07-14T12:23:22.238Z","author":"user","session":"915ea040-10d3-4f13-9cf2-ed8e5149babb"},{"id":"Q53","milestoneId":"M50","status":"answered","fields":{"question":"Boundary with G2/Q20: G2 already OWNS (by its locked Q20 decision) auto-wiring SizePacingFromBDP from operator-declared per-link bandwidth PLUS documenting BDP sizing - but that work is NOT yet materialized as tasks (G2's M14/M16/M17 are empty). Does G13 restrict item 2 to only the WARN/FAIL capacity-sanity guard + item 1 observability (deferring the actual auto-derive/auto-wire AND the item 3(a) BDP-sizing docs to G2), or should G13 own the auto-derive/docs too since G2 hasn't decomposed them?","context":"G2 is `planned`; its grounding records 'Q20 pacing = BOTH (wire SizePacingFromBDP from operator-declared per-link bandwidth + document measurement)'. G2 milestones M13 (tolerant startup) and M15 (hub failover) are done, but M14/M16/M17 hold no tasks - so the pacing-sizing + real-link + runbook scope exists as INTENT only. Your goal text says 'if item 2's auto-derive or item 3(a)'s sizing docs would duplicate a G2 task, reference the G2 task instead of restating it' - but there is no G2 task to reference yet. So the dedup must be resolved at the goal-ownership level: either (A) G13 stays operability-only (guard + metric + probe class) and G2 later owns the auto-derive + BDP docs, or (B) G13 absorbs the auto-derive + docs because they are prerequisite to G13's guard and G2 has not claimed them concretely.","suggestions":["A - G13 stays operability-only: capacity-sanity WARN/FAIL guard (item 2), wanbond_aggregation_engaged + offered-load metrics (item 1), and the probe/priority class (item 3(ii)). The per_path_capacity_fps auto-derive from link_bandwidth and the BDP-sizing docs (item 3(a)) remain G2/Q20 and are only REFERENCED here.","B - G13 owns auto-derive + BDP docs too (fold Q20's pacing-sizing into G13 since G2 left it undecomposed), and G2's Q20 pacing-sizing scope is retired/redirected to avoid duplication.","C - G13 owns the runtime guard AND the auto-derive wiring, but the BDP-sizing DOCUMENTATION (install.md §3a / item 3(a)) stays with G2's doc task."],"recommendation":"A: keep G13 crisply operability-scoped (guard + observability + probe class); leave the auto-derive wiring and BDP docs to G2's Q20 owner. If G2's pacing-sizing is later dropped, raise a follow-up rather than silently double-owning it now. This keeps the two goals' scopes disjoint and avoids re-planning G2's locked decision.","ledgerRefs":["goals:G13"],"answer":"as recommended"},"createdAt":"2026-07-14T12:16:52.104Z","updatedAt":"2026-07-14T12:24:07.462Z","author":"user","session":"915ea040-10d3-4f13-9cf2-ed8e5149babb"},{"id":"Q54","milestoneId":"M50","status":"answered","fields":{"question":"Item 1 observability - metric SHAPE and cardinality. The weighted scheduler is per-PEER (one scheduler per bound peer). For wanbond_aggregation_engaged (bool) and the offered-load-vs-threshold series, what label cardinality, and should engage/disengage ALSO emit a transition log line?","context":"The scheduler already holds all the state (s.aggregating, loadRate EWMA, EngageFraction/DisengageFraction*PerPathCapacity) but exposes NONE to the metrics Source/Collector (internal/metrics). Existing Prometheus series are labeled per-path (labelPath) or per-peer (labelPeer, used by FEC/resequencer). Aggregation is a per-peer-scheduler property, not per-path. Options for the numeric 'offered load vs threshold' visibility: expose (a) wanbond_offered_load_fps gauge, (b) the engage + disengage threshold gauges (fps), so a dashboard can plot load against both bands directly. Separately, liveness/failover already log transitions (path up/down, active-path change); an engage/disengage transition log line would give the same operability for the aggregation gate ('configured but inert' becomes visible in logs too, not only /metrics).","suggestions":["Per-peer label (labelPeer), matching the FEC/resequencer per-peer series: wanbond_aggregation_engaged{peer} plus wanbond_offered_load_fps{peer} and static wanbond_aggregation_engage_threshold_fps{peer}/..._disengage_threshold_fps{peer}; AND log an INFO transition on every engage<->disengage flip (parity with liveness transitions).","Same metrics but UNLABELED/global (assume the common single-peer edge) to keep cardinality minimal; still log transitions.","Bool gauge only (wanbond_aggregation_engaged{peer}) + transition log, WITHOUT the extra offered-load/threshold gauges (leave load visibility to logs)."],"recommendation":"Option 1: per-peer labels (consistent with existing per-peer series and correct on a multi-peer concentrator), expose offered-load + both threshold gauges so 'inert because load never crosses the band' is directly graphable, and add the engage/disengage transition log for parity with liveness. This most directly kills the 'configured but silently inert' blind spot you hit.","ledgerRefs":["goals:G13"],"answer":"as recommended"},"createdAt":"2026-07-14T12:17:05.130Z","updatedAt":"2026-07-14T12:24:22.467Z","author":"user","session":"915ea040-10d3-4f13-9cf2-ed8e5149babb"},{"id":"Q55","milestoneId":"M50","status":"answered","fields":{"question":"Acceptance-harness constraints: each task must be 'reproducible on a bandwidth-capped netns fixture'. Should per-task acceptance be netns e2e tests under `-tags e2e` (privileged, `just e2e`), or default-tag unprivileged unit/integration tests where the behavior allows? And does TestFixtureImpairment (T35) already support the three things these acceptances need - bandwidth cap, /metrics scrape of the new gauges, and injecting a competing/probe-starvation flow - or is extending the fixture itself a task in this plan?","context":"The definition-of-done here is `go test` GREEN plus `just lint` across default+e2e+realhosts tags (per project memory: a test-only view once reddened main's lint gate). Much of item 1 (metric wiring) and item 2 (config-load guard) is testable UNPRIVILEGED at the unit level (metrics.Exposition scrape helper exists; config.validate is pure); only the empirical engage/starvation behavior (aggregation actually engaging under load; the probe/pacer-overload starvation from item 3(ii)) needs the capped netns fixture. TestFixtureImpairment (test/e2e/fixture_impairment_test.go) exposes rateMbit/lossPct capping, and metrics.Fetch scrapes /metrics, but it is unclear whether it can drive a sustained overload that flips wanbond_aggregation_engaged AND concurrently observe probe/small-frame starvation - if not, a fixture-extension task is needed as a dependency.","suggestions":["Prefer unprivileged default-tag unit/integration acceptance wherever the behavior permits (metric registration+values, config-load guard), and use `-tags e2e` netns fixture acceptance ONLY for the empirical engage-under-load and pacer-starvation tasks; add an explicit fixture-extension task if TestFixtureImpairment can't yet drive sustained overload + observe probe starvation.","All acceptance via `-tags e2e` netns fixture for uniformity (heavier, privileged, but end-to-end).","Unit-only wherever possible and treat the empirical netns validations as report-only (M10/Q12-style, non-gating), mirroring G2's real-link tier."],"recommendation":"Option 1: push acceptance to the cheapest level that still verifies the behavior (unit for metric/guard, e2e only for the load-dependent effects), and make any needed TestFixtureImpairment extension an explicit up-front task other tasks depend on - so no task's acceptance is blocked on fixture capability that doesn't exist yet.","ledgerRefs":["goals:G13"],"answer":"All acceptance via `-tags e2e` netns fixture for uniformity (heavier, privileged, but end-to-end)."},"createdAt":"2026-07-14T12:17:19.822Z","updatedAt":"2026-07-14T12:24:40.993Z","author":"user","session":"915ea040-10d3-4f13-9cf2-ed8e5149babb"}],"goals":[{"id":"G13","milestoneId":"M50","status":"planning","fields":{"title":"Operability & safety for weighted aggregation + pacing","description":"Plan (task DAG + acceptance) the operability gaps found while testing policy="weighted" with pacing on and off. Both functionally worked — the single flow striped across both paths — but they are hard to operate safely.

1. **Aggregation-engagement observability.** Weighted mode sits silently in primary-only until offered load exceeds `engage_fraction × per_path_capacity_fps`. With the default `per_path_capacity_fps = 10000` (~100+ Mbps/path) aggregation NEVER engages on a low-throughput / CPU-bound edge — the operator sees `policy="weighted"` but single-path behavior and no signal why. Add a metric + log: `wanbond_aggregation_engaged` (bool) and the current EWMA offered-load vs the engage/disengage thresholds, so "configured but inert" is directly visible. (Empirically: had to hand-set `per_path_capacity_fps = 250` to trigger aggregation at all.)

content.text: {"item":{"id":"T142","milestoneId":"M52","status":"planned","fields":{"headline":"Hard-fail config load when declared link_bandwidth proves weighted aggregation can never engage","description":"Q52 hard-fail arm (Option 3), scoped by Q53 to the GUARD ONLY (G2/Q20 owns per_path_capacity_fps auto-derive + BDP-sizing docs — reference install.md 3a, do NOT restate; note deriveWeightedPacingFromBDP already exists in internal/config/config.go:955). In internal/config, AFTER normalize (so deriveWeightedPacingFromBDP and applyDefaults have produced the EFFECTIVE EngageFraction/PerPathCapacityFPS): when scheduler.policy=\"weighted\" and a path declares link_bandwidth (Path.LinkBandwidthBitsPerSec), compute impliedCapacityFPS = LinkBandwidthBitsPerSec / (8 * defaultAvgWireFrameBytes) using the SAME avg-wire-frame constant and math SizePacingFromBDP uses (so the guard and the BDP derive can never disagree). If EngageFraction*PerPathCapacityFPS > impliedCapacityFPS for any declared path (aggregation can mathematically never engage at line rate), Load fails fast with an actionable error naming the path and all three numbers (declared bandwidth, implied capacity fps, engage-threshold fps) and the fixes (lower per_path_capacity_fps, enable pacing to auto-derive it, or correct link_bandwidth). Interaction to respect: with pacing ENABLED + declared bandwidth the capacity is auto-derived (raw knobs mutually exclusive, config.go:972), so the guard chiefly bites when pacing is DISABLED (derive no-ops, synthetic 10000 default stands) or knobs are explicit. Document the new failure mode in docs/install.md's config-error list in the same change (AGENTS.md docs-sync).","acceptance":"-tags e2e under `just e2e` (fixture builds+runs the binary): (i) a daemon launched with policy=\"weighted\", link_bandwidth=\"8mbit\" (+link_rtt), pacing disabled, and default per_path_capacity_fps REFUSES to start, exiting non-zero with the actionable error naming the implied capacity fps and engage-threshold fps; (ii) the same config with per_path_capacity_fps lowered so EngageFraction*capacity <= impliedCapacityFPS starts and establishes the tunnel. `go test` GREEN, `just lint` (default+e2e+realhosts) GREEN.","suggestedModel":"standard","ledgerRefs":["goals:G13"]},"createdAt":"2026-07-14T12:40:34.697Z","updatedAt":"2026-07-14T12:40:34.697Z","author":"opus-4.8[1m]","session":"915ea040-10d3-4f13-9cf2-ed8e5149babb"}}

content.text: {"item":{"id":"T143","milestoneId":"M53","status":"planned","fields":{"headline":"Expose WeightedScheduler aggregation-gate snapshot and log engage/disengage transitions","description":"Item 1 + Q54, sched-package half. internal/sched/weighted.go holds the whole gate state under s.mu — s.aggregating, the EWMA loadRate (fps), and the thresholds EngageFraction*PerPathCapacity / DisengageFraction*PerPathCapacity — but exposes none of it and flips the gate silently in updateGateLocked. (1) Add a mutex-guarded snapshot accessor on *WeightedScheduler, e.g. AggregationSnapshot() returning {Aggregating bool, OfferedLoadFPS, EngageThresholdFPS, DisengageThresholdFPS}, as the read seam the metrics plumbing consumes. (2) In updateGateLocked emit an INFO transition log on every s.aggregating flip, with structured fields (from/to, load_fps, engage_threshold_fps, disengage_threshold_fps) — parity with the liveness transition log (internal/telemetry/liveness.go transition) and the existing 'scheduler active path change' one-shot-on-change pattern (setActiveLocked), so a saturated Pick path does NOT log per-frame. Active-backup has no gate and is untouched. Pure sched-package change; NO metrics wiring here (next task). Document the new log line in docs/design.md's scheduler section in the same change (AGENTS.md docs-sync).","acceptance":"-tags e2e under `just e2e`: weighted-policy daemon with per_path_capacity_fps=250 (the empirical repro value) on the netns fixture; using the harness overload driver, offered load pushed above engage_fraction*250 makes the log capturer observe exactly one 'scheduler aggregation engaged' INFO line with the structured fields; stopping the load yields the 'scheduler aggregation disengaged' line within the collapse-dwell + EWMA-tau budget (wait derived from configured knobs, no magic sleeps), parity with 'scheduler active path change'. `go test` GREEN, `just lint` (default+e2e+realhosts) GREEN.","suggestedModel":"standard","dependsOn":["T141"],"ledgerRefs":["goals:G13"]},"createdAt":"2026-07-14T12:40:52.205Z","updatedAt":"2026-07-14T12:40:52.205Z","author":"opus-4.8[1m]","session":"915ea040-10d3-4f13-9cf2-ed8e5149babb"}}

content.text: {"item":{"id":"T144","milestoneId":"M52","status":"planned","fields":{"headline":"Startup WARN and wanbond_weighted_capacity_sane gauge when weighted capacity is unverifiable","description":"Q52 WARN arm. When policy=\"weighted\" and link_bandwidth is NOT declared on any path, startup must NEVER be blocked: config load computes a capacity-sanity verdict (sane-verified when every path declares bandwidth and the hard-fail guard passed; unverifiable when no bandwidth is declared). In the unverifiable case the daemon logs ONE actionable startup WARN (message: declare link_bandwidth per path so capacity can be checked, or verify per_path_capacity_fps against the BDP sizing in install.md 3a — REFERENCE, do not restate, the G2-owned sizing docs). The metrics endpoint exposes a STATIC unlabeled gauge wanbond_weighted_capacity_sane: 1 when verified sane, 0 when unverifiable; the family is ABSENT entirely under non-weighted policy. Plumb the verdict from the loaded config through internal/device to collector registration as a static registered gauge alongside the Source-driven collector (it is config-derived, NOT per-peer, hence unlabeled and exempt from the labelPeer back-compat rule). Export the metric name as a constant. Update the metrics reference (README.md/docs/design.md) and docs/install.md in the same change.","acceptance":"-tags e2e under `just e2e`: (a) weighted daemon with NO link_bandwidth starts on the fixture, the log capturer finds exactly one capacity-sanity WARN line, and metrics.Fetch reads wanbond_weighted_capacity_sane == 0; (b) the same config with link_bandwidth declared on all paths (and guard-consistent capacity) starts with NO such WARN and the gauge reads 1; (c) under active-backup policy the family is absent. `go test` GREEN, `just lint` (default+e2e+realhosts) GREEN.","suggestedModel":"standard","dependsOn":["T142"],"ledgerRefs":["goals:G13"]},"createdAt":"2026-07-14T12:41:01.308Z","updatedAt":"2026-07-14T12:41:01.308Z","author":"opus-4.8[1m]","session":"915ea040-10d3-4f13-9cf2-ed8e5149babb"}}

content.text: {"item":{"id":"T145","milestoneId":"M54","status":"planned","fields":{"headline":"Reserve probe headroom in the weighted pacer: exempt-but-charged probe accounting","description":"Item 3(ii) + Q51 (PROBE-frame protection only; inner-ICMP explicitly OUT of scope). GROUNDING (load-bearing, confirmed by BOTH candidate planners): wanbond's own PROBE frames (frame.KindProbe) do NOT traverse scheduler.Pick — emitProbes (internal/bind/probe.go) writes them directly to each path socket, bypassing Send->Pick->token-bucket. So a ClassControl-style pacer EXEMPTION does not apply; the failure mode is that the pacer budgets ZERO headroom for probes, so a pace sized at ~link rate lets paced DATA + the probe stream (plus reflected echoes) oversubscribe the link, building the standing qdisc queue that delays/drops probes past DownAfter (1200ms, internal/telemetry/liveness.go) -> spurious path-DOWN / failover flap. REPRODUCE-FIRST: land a failing -tags e2e that observes the spurious path-down under sustained overload BEFORE the fix (confirm it fails for THIS reason). Then implement exempt-but-charged probe accounting: add a small optional interface (e.g. ProbeBudget{AccountProbe(pathIdx int)}) implemented by *WeightedScheduler — deduct one token from the path's pacing bucket per emitted probe WITHOUT ever shedding or delaying the probe (strict priority: bucket may briefly go negative / pre-drain so subsequent ClassData Picks yield) — and call it from the bind's probe emission AND the echo-reflection write in dispatchInbound (symmetric). ClassControl semantics stay EXACTLY as D22 (exempt AND uncharged) — do not re-plan. Codify the three-tier invariant in the FrameClass/Pick contract comments (internal/sched/scheduler.go) and docs/design.md priority model: ClassControl exempt-uncharged, KindProbe exempt-but-charged, ClassData fully paced.","acceptance":"-tags e2e under `just e2e` (reproduce-first): weighted + pacing daemon on a rate-capped netns path with pace sized at ~the link rate; the harness driver sustains ClassData overload >= 2x pacing capacity for >= 10s (> 8x DownAfter). The regression test FAILS (observes a 'path liveness transition' to=down and/or wanbond_path_up->0 for the loaded path) with the probe accounting disabled/absent, and PASSES with it enabled: ZERO to=down transitions during the overload window, 'scheduler pacer shedding' lines ARE present (overload proven real), and wanbond_path_rtt_seconds for the loaded path stays below the DownAfter threshold throughout. `go test` GREEN (race detector per repo default), `just lint` (default+e2e+realhosts) GREEN.","suggestedModel":"frontier","dependsOn":["T141"],"ledgerRefs":["goals:G13"]},"createdAt":"2026-07-14T12:41:14.250Z","updatedAt":"2026-07-14T12:41:14.250Z","author":"opus-4.8[1m]","session":"915ea040-10d3-4f13-9cf2-ed8e5149babb"}}

183	// NewWeighted builds the weighted-aggregation scheduler over parallel health and
184	// quality slices in priority order (index 0 = preferred primary). health and quality
185	// must be the same length; a quality element MAY be nil (that path then gets a
186	// neutral weight until a real PathQuality is wired). It fails fast on an empty or
187	// length-mismatched set, a nil health element, a nil dependency, or an invalid config.
188	func NewWeighted(health []PathHealth, quality []PathQuality, cfg WeightedConfig, clock telemetry.Clock, logger log.Logger) (*WeightedScheduler, error) {
189		if len(health) == 0 {
190			return nil, fmt.Errorf("sched: at least one path health source is required")
191		}
192		if len(quality) != len(health) {
193			return nil, fmt.Errorf("sched: quality set (%d) must be one entry per health source (%d)", len(quality), len(health))
194		}
195		for i, h := range health {
196			if h == nil {
197				return nil, fmt.Errorf("sched: path %d health source is nil", i)
198			}
199		}
200		if clock == nil {
201			return nil, fmt.Errorf("sched: clock is required")
202		}
203		if logger == nil {
204			return nil, fmt.Errorf("sched: logger is required")
205		}
206		if err := cfg.validate(); err != nil {
207			return nil, err
208		}
209		return &WeightedScheduler{
210			health:  append([]PathHealth(nil), health...),
211			quality: append([]PathQuality(nil), quality...),
212			clock:   clock,
213			cfg:     cfg,
214			log:     logger.Component("sched"),
215			current: make([]float64, len(health)),
216			tokens:  fullBuckets(len(health), cfg.PacingBurst),
217			active:  -1,
218		}, nil
219	}

221	// SetFEC installs the P3+ redundancy policy (nil clears it). It is the documented
222	// extension point for FEC-aware path selection; the current single-path Pick does
223	// not consult it (redundant transmission needs the P3 Bind fan-out), but
224	// RedundantPaths exposes it so a P3 Bind can query the additional paths per frame.
225	func (s *WeightedScheduler) SetFEC(fec FECPolicy) {
226		s.mu.Lock()
227		defer s.mu.Unlock()
228		s.fec = fec
229	}

231	// RedundantPaths reports the ADDITIONAL path indices a frame scheduled onto chosen
232	// should also egress on, per the installed FECPolicy over the current eligible set.
233	// It returns nil when no policy is installed (no FEC). It is the P3 hook a redundant-
234	// transmission Bind would call alongside Pick; today it is a documented, unit-tested
235	// seam with no in-tree caller on the hot path.
236	func (s *WeightedScheduler) RedundantPaths(chosen int) []int {
237		s.mu.Lock()
238		defer s.mu.Unlock()
239		if s.fec == nil {

245	// Pick selects the path for the NEXT frame and advances all per-frame distribution
246	// state (offered-load meter, aggregation gate, pacing buckets, round-robin credits).
247	// It returns a negative value when no path is eligible OR when every eligible path
248	// is paced out (the frame is dropped rather than queued — pacing bounds egress and,
249	// by dropping the overflow, bounds the send backlog). It is safe for concurrent
250	// callers.
251	func (s *WeightedScheduler) Pick(class FrameClass) int {
252		now := s.clock.Now()
253		s.mu.Lock()
254		defer s.mu.Unlock()
255	
256		// Capture the inter-sample idle gap BEFORE observeLoadLocked advances lastLoad: a
257		// long gap since the previous offered frame is itself the strongest evidence of low
258		// load, and the collapse hysteresis must count it (criticism #1 — otherwise an
259		// abrupt overload stop leaves the gate engaged across the whole idle span and the
260		// next low burst dribbles onto the metered path).
261		gap := s.loadGapLocked(now)
262		s.recomputeLocked(now)       // liveness refresh + eager-failover log
263		s.observeLoadLocked(now)     // this Pick is one offered frame (decays across the gap)
264		s.updateGateLocked(now, gap) // engage/disengage aggregation (hysteresis, idle-aware)
265		s.refillLocked(now)          // top up pacing buckets
266	
267		eligible := s.eligibleLocked()
268		if len(eligible) == 0 {
269			return PickNone
270		}
271		if !s.aggregating {
272			// Data-thrift: at low load only the primary (best eligible) carries traffic;
273			// the metered backup stays idle. A path-down excludes it from eligible, so
274			// eligible[0] is the surviving highest-priority path — failover is preserved.
275			return s.serveLocked(now, eligible[0], class)
276		}
277		weights := s.weightsLocked(eligible)
278		return s.selectAggregatingLocked(now, eligible, weights, class)
279	}

282	// 0 before the first sample. Caller holds s.mu.

3	import (

8	// Smoothing constants for the RTT/jitter estimator. They mirror the RFC 6298
9	// SRTT/RTTVAR recursion (alpha = 1/8, beta = 1/4): srtt tracks the smoothed
10	// round-trip time and rttvar the smoothed absolute deviation of samples from it,
11	// which is the jitter this codec reports.
12	const (
13		rttAlpha = 0.125
14		rttBeta  = 0.25
15	
16		// defaultLossWindow is the sequence-space width of the windowed loss estimator
17		// (per-path over probe-echo ProbeSeq gaps; connection-scoped over the outer-seq
18		// in ConnLoss) when a caller passes a non-positive window.
19		defaultLossWindow = 512
20	)
21	
22	// Estimate is a point-in-time snapshot of a path's measured quality. RTT,
23	// Jitter, and Loss all derive from the path's ACTIVE probe stream: RTT/Jitter
24	// from probe-echo timings, and Loss from gaps in the probe-echo ProbeSeq (NOT
25	// the connection-global outer DATA sequence — see the Estimator doc below and
26	// ConnLoss).
27	type Estimate struct {
28		// RTT is the smoothed round-trip time.
29		RTT time.Duration
30		// Jitter is the smoothed absolute deviation of RTT samples (RFC 6298 RTTVAR).
31		Jitter time.Duration
32		// Loss is the per-path loss fraction in [0,1] over the current probe-echo
33		// ProbeSeq window.
34		Loss float64
35	}
36	
37	// Estimator fuses per-path quality signals: an EWMA RTT and jitter estimator
38	// (ObserveRTT) and a windowed per-path loss estimator, BOTH fed by the path's
39	// active probe stream. Per-path loss is derived from gaps in the ProbeSeq of
40	// received probe echoes (ObserveProbeEcho), NOT from the outer DATA sequence:
41	// the outer-seq is connection-global — a single sequence the send scheduler
42	// stripes across all paths (T12) and the receiver resequences into one global
43	// order (T18) — so counting a single path's outer-seq gaps would read scheduler
44	// striping (and a mid-stream path attach) as loss. Connection-scoped outer-seq
45	// loss is a separate, correctly-scoped metric: see ConnLoss.
46	//
47	// Estimator holds no clock, does no I/O, and is NOT safe for concurrent use; its
48	// owner (Prober) serializes access. It is exercised directly on synthetic traces.
49	type Estimator struct {
50		haveRTT bool
51		srtt    float64 // nanoseconds
52		rttvar  float64 // nanoseconds
53		loss    *lossWindow
54	}
55	
56	// NewEstimator builds an Estimator whose per-path loss estimate is computed over
57	// a trailing window of lossWindow probe echoes (defaultLossWindow when
58	// non-positive).
59	func NewEstimator(lossWindow int) *Estimator {
60		return &Estimator{loss: newLossWindow(lossWindow)}
61	}
62	
63	// ObserveRTT folds one round-trip-time sample into the smoothed RTT and jitter.
64	// The first sample seeds srtt directly and rttvar at half the sample, per RFC
65	// 6298.
66	func (e *Estimator) ObserveRTT(sample time.Duration) {
67		s := float64(sample)
68		if !e.haveRTT {
69			e.srtt = s
70			e.rttvar = s / 2
71			e.haveRTT = true
72			return
73		}
74		d := e.srtt - s
75		if d < 0 {
76			d = -d
77		}
78		e.rttvar = (1-rttBeta)*e.rttvar + rttBeta*d
79		e.srtt = (1-rttAlpha)*e.srtt + rttAlpha*s
80	}

82	// ObserveProbeEcho folds one received probe echo's ProbeSeq into the per-path
83	// loss estimate. The prober assigns ProbeSeq densely (0,1,2,…) on this path, so a
84	// gap in received echo seqs is a probe that did not round-trip — i.e. per-path
85	// loss. Echoes may arrive out of order; a late arrival within the window
86	// retroactively fills its gap, and a duplicate is idempotent.
87	func (e *Estimator) ObserveProbeEcho(seq uint64) {
88		e.loss.observe(seq)
89	}

92	func (e *Estimator) Estimate() Estimate {
93		return Estimate{
94			RTT:    time.Duration(e.srtt),
95			Jitter: time.Duration(e.rttvar),
96			Loss:   e.loss.fraction(),
97		}

100	// lossWindow is a sliding-window loss estimator over a monotonic sequence space.
101	// It tracks, for the trailing win sequence numbers ending at the highest seen,
102	// which were received; loss is the unfilled fraction of that window. It never
103	// inspects payloads — only sequence presence — so a burst of missing sequence
104	// numbers reads as loss without any per-packet state beyond the ring.
105	//
106	// The window is bounded below by the FIRST observed sequence, not by zero, so a
107	// stream that starts (or a receiver that attaches) mid-sequence does not count
108	// the never-seen prefix as loss.

111		recv    []bool
112		first   uint64
113		highest uint64

117	func newLossWindow(win int) *lossWindow {
118		if win <= 0 {

120		}
121		return &lossWindow{win: win, recv: make([]bool, win)}
122	}

124	func (w *lossWindow) slot(seq uint64) int { return int(seq % uint64(w.win)) }

126	func (w *lossWindow) observe(seq uint64) {
127		if !w.have {
128			w.have = true
129			w.first = seq
130			w.highest = seq
131			for i := range w.recv {
132				w.recv[i] = false
133			}
134			w.recv[w.slot(seq)] = true
135			return
136		}
137		if seq > w.highest {
138			// The positions (highest, seq] are newly exposed by the window advancing;
139			// mark them missing, then record the one that actually arrived.
140			if seq-w.highest >= uint64(w.win) {
141				for i := range w.recv {
142					w.recv[i] = false
143				}
144			} else {
145				for k := w.highest + 1; k <= seq; k++ {
146					w.recv[w.slot(k)] = false
147				}
148			}
149			w.recv[w.slot(seq)] = true
150			w.highest = seq
151			return
152		}
153		// Late/reordered arrival: fill its slot only if it still falls in the window.
154		if w.highest-seq < uint64(w.win) {
155			w.recv[w.slot(seq)] = true
156		}
157	}

159	func (w *lossWindow) fraction() float64 {
160		if !w.have {
161			return 0
162		}
163		lower := uint64(0)
164		if w.highest+1 > uint64(w.win) {
165			lower = w.highest + 1 - uint64(w.win)
166		}
167		if lower < w.first {
168			lower = w.first // never charge loss for sequences before the first observed
169		}
170		n := w.highest - lower + 1
171		missing := 0
172		for k := lower; k <= w.highest; k++ {
173			if !w.recv[w.slot(k)] {
174				missing++
175			}
176		}
177		return float64(missing) / float64(n)
178	}

182	// across every path (T12) and the receiver resequences into one global order

184	// frames would read scheduler striping as loss. Feed it EVERY received DATA

10	// emitProbes performs one probe cadence step: for every currently-open path it
11	// emits one authenticated PROBE frame (IsEcho=false) to that path's learned/
12	// configured remote, then Ticks that path's Prober so liveness advances against
13	// the injected clock. A path without a known remote yet is still Ticked (so a
14	// silent path is detected Down) but nothing is sent — there is nowhere to send.
15	//
16	// Concurrency mirrors Send: the path/prober set is snapshotted under m.mu, then
17	// released before any socket I/O, so emission neither holds the lock across a
18	// syscall nor blocks the lock-free receive fast path. A concurrent Close closes
19	// the sockets out from under the snapshot; the resulting write error is benign
20	// (teardown) and dropped. It is a no-op when the bind has no probers or is closed.
21	func (m *Multipath) emitProbes() {
22		m.mu.Lock()
23		if len(m.paths) == 0 || m.probers == nil {
24			m.mu.Unlock()
25			return
26		}
27		type target struct {
28			ps *peerPathState
29			pr *telemetry.Prober
30		}
31		// Probe EVERY bound peer's paths (T93): a concentrator initiates its own probe stream to
32		// each edge over that edge-peer's per-(peer,path) prober, so every peer's liveness/RTT is
33		// measured for its OWN scheduler. On the single-peer edge/hub m.peers holds only the
34		// primary, so this is byte-identical to the pre-split single-peer sweep.
35		targets := make([]target, 0, len(m.paths))
36		for _, p := range m.peers {
37			for _, ps := range p.paths {
38				if ps.prober == nil {
39					continue
40				}
41				targets = append(targets, target{ps: ps, pr: ps.prober})
42			}
43		}
44		m.mu.Unlock()
45	
46		for _, t := range targets {
47			if remote, ok := t.ps.getRemote(); ok {
48				if raw, err := t.pr.SendProbe(); err == nil {
49					// UDP writes are goroutine-safe; this races no in-flight Send.
50					if _, werr := t.ps.conn.WriteToUDPAddrPort(raw, remote); werr == nil {
51						// True-wire-volume accounting (D48): a PROBE frame is real egress
52						// traffic, so it counts toward txBytes exactly like a DATA/PARITY
53						// write — only on a nil write error, matching the Send hot path.
54						t.ps.txBytes.Add(uint64(len(raw)))
55					}
56				}
57			}
58			t.pr.Tick()
59		}
60		// Eager failover nudge (defect D18): recompute the scheduler's active egress path
61		// each probe cadence so a liveness DOWN switches egress even when no application
62		// Send is driving Pick. This is the timer-driven companion to the receive-tick
63		// nudge (see tickLivenessFromReceive) — under CPU starvation this loop's ticker
64		// lags, which is why the receive-tick path carries the guarantee, but when the
65		// loop does run it keeps the selection fresh independent of egress traffic.
66		m.nudgeSchedulerActive()

70	// interval until the returned stop function is invoked. The caller (device.Up)
71	// starts it AFTER the engine has opened the bind and stops it BEFORE Close, so the
72	// loop only runs while the sockets exist (emitProbes is a safe no-op if it races a

75	// The cadence uses a wall-clock ticker because it is production timing glue, not
76	// liveness logic: every liveness decision the loop drives runs through the
77	// injected telemetry.Clock the Probers hold (SendProbe stamps it, Tick reads it),
78	// so tests drive emitProbes directly against a fake clock and never start this
79	// goroutine. It is a no-op (returning a no-op stopper) when the bind has no
80	// probers or interval <= 0.
81	func (m *Multipath) StartProbeLoop(interval time.Duration) (stop func()) {
82		if m.probers == nil || interval <= 0 {
83			return func() {}
84		}
85		// Arm the receive-path liveness sweep at the same cadence (D15): a receiver may
86		// now advance liveness when the timer goroutine below is starved under load.
87		m.sweepIntervalNanos.Store(int64(interval))
88		done := make(chan struct{})
89		go func() {
90			ticker := time.NewTicker(interval)
91			defer ticker.Stop()
92			for {
93				select {
94				case <-done:
95					return
96				case <-ticker.C:
97					m.emitProbes()
98				}
99			}
100		}()
101		var once sync.Once
102		return func() { once.Do(func() { close(done) }) }
103	}

10	// emitProbes performs one probe cadence step: for every currently-open path it
11	// emits one authenticated PROBE frame (IsEcho=false) to that path's learned/
12	// configured remote, then Ticks that path's Prober so liveness advances against
13	// the injected clock. A path without a known remote yet is still Ticked (so a
14	// silent path is detected Down) but nothing is sent — there is nowhere to send.
15	//
16	// Concurrency mirrors Send: the path/prober set is snapshotted under m.mu, then
17	// released before any socket I/O, so emission neither holds the lock across a
18	// syscall nor blocks the lock-free receive fast path. A concurrent Close closes
19	// the sockets out from under the snapshot; the resulting write error is benign
20	// (teardown) and dropped. It is a no-op when the bind has no probers or is closed.
21	func (m *Multipath) emitProbes() {
22		m.mu.Lock()
23		if len(m.paths) == 0 || m.probers == nil {
24			m.mu.Unlock()
25			return
26		}
27		type target struct {
28			ps *peerPathState
29			pr *telemetry.Prober
30		}
31		// Probe EVERY bound peer's paths (T93): a concentrator initiates its own probe stream to
32		// each edge over that edge-peer's per-(peer,path) prober, so every peer's liveness/RTT is
33		// measured for its OWN scheduler. On the single-peer edge/hub m.peers holds only the
34		// primary, so this is byte-identical to the pre-split single-peer sweep.
35		targets := make([]target, 0, len(m.paths))
36		for _, p := range m.peers {
37			for _, ps := range p.paths {
38				if ps.prober == nil {
39					continue
40				}
41				targets = append(targets, target{ps: ps, pr: ps.prober})
42			}
43		}
44		m.mu.Unlock()
45	
46		for _, t := range targets {
47			if remote, ok := t.ps.getRemote(); ok {
48				if raw, err := t.pr.SendProbe(); err == nil {
49					// UDP writes are goroutine-safe; this races no in-flight Send.
50					if _, werr := t.ps.conn.WriteToUDPAddrPort(raw, remote); werr == nil {
51						// True-wire-volume accounting (D48): a PROBE frame is real egress
52						// traffic, so it counts toward txBytes exactly like a DATA/PARITY
53						// write — only on a nil write error, matching the Send hot path.
54						t.ps.txBytes.Add(uint64(len(raw)))
55					}
56				}
57			}
58			t.pr.Tick()
59		}
60		// Eager failover nudge (defect D18): recompute the scheduler's active egress path
61		// each probe cadence so a liveness DOWN switches egress even when no application
62		// Send is driving Pick. This is the timer-driven companion to the receive-tick
63		// nudge (see tickLivenessFromReceive) — under CPU starvation this loop's ticker
64		// lags, which is why the receive-tick path carries the guarantee, but when the
65		// loop does run it keeps the selection fresh independent of egress traffic.
66		m.nudgeSchedulerActive()

70	// interval until the returned stop function is invoked. The caller (device.Up)
71	// starts it AFTER the engine has opened the bind and stops it BEFORE Close, so the
72	// loop only runs while the sockets exist (emitProbes is a safe no-op if it races a

76	// liveness logic: every liveness decision the loop drives runs through the

82		if m.probers == nil || interval <= 0 {
83			return func() {}
84		}

86		// now advance liveness when the timer goroutine below is starved under load.
87		m.sweepIntervalNanos.Store(int64(interval))
88		done := make(chan struct{})
89		go func() {
90			ticker := time.NewTicker(interval)
91			defer ticker.Stop()
92			for {
93				select {
94				case <-done:
95					return
96				case <-ticker.C:
97					m.emitProbes()
98				}
99			}
100		}()
101		var once sync.Once
102		return func() { once.Do(func() { close(done) }) }
103	}

3	import (
4		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/telemetry"

7	// Negative Pick sentinels. Pick returns a NON-NEGATIVE path index when a datagram
8	// should egress, and one of these when it should not. They are distinct so the Send
9	// path can tell a genuine outage (no eligible path) apart from a transient pacer
10	// shedding a frame while every path is healthy — the two must not read the same in
11	// operator logs or the e2e log-grep harness.
12	const (
13		// PickNone means no path is currently eligible (a total outage among this
14		// scheduler's paths). The Bind maps it to "no healthy path".
15		PickNone = -1
16		// PickPaced means eligible paths EXIST but every one is momentarily paced out, so
17		// this frame is shed (dropped) to bound egress and the send backlog. The paths are
18		// healthy — this is deliberate rate limiting, NOT an outage. Only a pacing-enabled
19		// weighted scheduler ever returns it.
20		PickPaced = -2
21	)
22	
23	// FrameClass tags an outbound datagram with the traffic class the pacer must treat
24	// it as (defect D22). The multipath Bind classifies each frame from the inner
25	// WireGuard message type and passes the class to Pick so a pacing scheduler can give
26	// WireGuard control frames (handshake init/response, cookie reply, keepalive) a
27	// different admission policy than bulk data. Pick() has no other frame-type
28	// visibility, so this is the ONLY channel by which frame-type reaches the pacer.
29	type FrameClass uint8
30	
31	const (
32		// ClassData is bulk/opaque data (a WireGuard transport datagram carrying tunnelled
33		// payload). It is fully paced: under a weighted scheduler with pacing enabled it is
34		// subject to the per-path token buckets and is shed (PickPaced) when they are empty.
35		ClassData FrameClass = iota
36		// ClassControl is a WireGuard control frame — handshake initiation/response, cookie
37		// reply, or keepalive. It is pacing-EXEMPT: a pacing scheduler never sheds it for an
38		// empty token bucket and never spends a data token for it, so sustained bulk overload
39		// cannot starve rekey (defect D22). Control frames originate from OUR OWN WireGuard
40		// engine on the send path (an attacker cannot inject them here), and WireGuard's own
41		// state machine bounds their rate, so exempting them from the data pace is safe.
42		ClassControl
43	)
44	
45	// Scheduler is the send-side path-selection policy the multipath Bind consults
46	// for every outbound datagram. It is the extension seam for the send scheduler:
47	// active-backup failover (ActiveBackup, the P1 MVP) is one implementation; the
48	// later weighted / FEC-aware policy (T21) is a DIFFERENT implementation of this
49	// same interface, swapped in at the composition root with no Bind change.
50	type Scheduler interface {
51		// Pick returns the index of the path the next datagram must egress on, in
52		// the path priority order the scheduler was built over (index 0 = preferred
53		// primary). It returns a negative value when no datagram should be sent this
54		// call; the negative value is one of the Pick* sentinels (PickNone for no
55		// eligible path, PickPaced for a healthy-but-paced-out shed), so the caller can
56		// tell a genuine outage apart from deliberate rate limiting. Pick is safe for
57		// concurrent callers on the Bind's send path.
58		//
59		// class is the frame's traffic class (defect D22): a pacing scheduler exempts
60		// ClassControl (WireGuard handshake/keepalive) from the data token buckets so
61		// bulk overload cannot shed control frames and starve rekey, while ClassData is
62		// fully paced. A non-pacing scheduler (active-backup) ignores class.
63		//
64		// Pick MAY be stateful: a weighted/aggregating scheduler advances its
65		// distribution bookkeeping (deficit/round-robin credits, pacing tokens, offered-
66		// load meter) on every call, so ONE Pick consumes ONE frame-selection slot.
67		// Callers that only want the liveness-derived active set refreshed — without
68		// perturbing distribution — MUST call Recompute, not Pick (see the T40 eager-
69		// failover nudge in the multipath Bind).
70		Pick(class FrameClass) int
71	
72		// Recompute refreshes the scheduler's liveness-derived selection state (which
73		// paths are eligible, which is the active primary) from the current PathHealth,
74		// and logs any active-path transition, WITHOUT selecting or consuming a frame
75		// slot: it advances no distribution/pacing/load state. It is the split-out
76		// "recompute the eligible/active set from liveness" half of the old Pick, so the
77		// eager-failover nudge (defect D18/T40) can drive an egress-lull failover recompute
78		// without stealing a weighted-distribution slot the way a spurious Pick would. For
79		// an idempotent scheduler (active-backup) it is exactly Pick without the return;
80		// for a stateful one (weighted) it is strictly the non-consuming subset. It is
81		// safe for concurrent callers and never calls back into the Bind.

83	}
84	
85	// PathQuality is the per-path measured-quality source the weighted scheduler reads
86	// to derive distribution weights: the read side of the T13 telemetry Estimate
87	// (RTT/Jitter/Loss), ANALOGOUS to PathHealth but exposing the quality snapshot
88	// rather than the up/down verdict. *telemetry.Prober satisfies BOTH PathHealth and
89	// PathQuality (its Estimate() is mutex-guarded, so it meets the same concurrency

95		Estimate() telemetry.Estimate
96	}

98	// DynamicScheduler is a Scheduler whose path set can change at runtime (T30): a
99	// path may be admitted or dropped WITHOUT rebuilding the scheduler or disturbing
100	// the active selection of the surviving paths. The multipath Bind holds its
101	// scheduler as a plain Scheduler and type-asserts to this richer interface only
102	// when a runtime path add/remove is requested, so the Scheduler seam (and the
103	// T21 weighted policy that will implement it) is preserved: a scheduler that
104	// does not support dynamic membership simply cannot back a reloadable bind.
105	//
106	// The index space is the SAME priority-ordered index Pick returns, and the Bind
107	// keeps its own path slice index-aligned with this scheduler's path list, so an
108	// index returned by Pick always addresses the matching path. Both methods are
109	// safe for concurrent callers.
110	type DynamicScheduler interface {
111		Scheduler
112		// AddPath admits h as a NEW lowest-priority path (highest index) and returns
113		// that index. Appending at the tail cannot change any existing path's index
114		// nor steal the active selection from a higher-priority survivor: an
115		// active-backup policy only ever selects the new path once every
116		// higher-priority path is down. h must be non-nil.
117		AddPath(h PathHealth) (int, error)
118		// RemovePath drops the path at index i (shifting higher indices down by one)
119		// and repairs the cached selection so a surviving path keeps being selected
120		// and, if the dropped path was the active one, egress fails over to the best
121		// remaining path on the next Pick. i must be in range.
122		RemovePath(i int) error
123		// SetPaths replaces the ENTIRE path/health list with health (priority order,
124		// index 0 = preferred primary) and clears any cached selection, so the next
125		// Pick re-derives the active path from live liveness. The Bind calls it on
126		// every Open to re-align the scheduler's membership with the path slice it
127		// rebuilds from the current definitions — the single reconciliation point that
128		// makes a runtime path add/remove survive a Close→Open cycle index-aligned
129		// (T30). It is safe for concurrent callers; health must be non-empty and hold
130		// no nil element.
131		SetPaths(health []PathHealth) error
132	}
133	
134	// PathHealth is the per-path liveness the scheduler consumes: the read side of
135	// the T13 telemetry liveness state machine, so the scheduler reads exactly the
136	// up/down verdict T13 already computes (with its own probe-loss hysteresis)
137	// rather than duplicating detection here.
138	//
139	// Concurrency contract: Pick runs on the Bind send path CONCURRENTLY with the
140	// probe/timer goroutines that mutate liveness, so a production PathHealth MUST
141	// be INTERNALLY SYNCHRONIZED. *telemetry.Prober satisfies this — its State()
142	// takes the Prober's mutex (probe.go). A bare *telemetry.Liveness does NOT: it
143	// is not independently synchronized (probe.go: "must be reached only through

145	// wiring a live, probe-driven *Liveness directly here is a data race — use a
146	// *Prober or an explicitly-locked adapter instead. (The sched unit tests drive

149	type PathHealth interface {
150		State() telemetry.PathState
151	}
152	
153	// AlwaysUp is a PathHealth that reports StateUp unconditionally. It is the
154	// composition-root placeholder used until the probe-transport wiring (a

3	import (

7	// Negative Pick sentinels. Pick returns a NON-NEGATIVE path index when a datagram
8	// should egress, and one of these when it should not. They are distinct so the Send
9	// path can tell a genuine outage (no eligible path) apart from a transient pacer
10	// shedding a frame while every path is healthy — the two must not read the same in
11	// operator logs or the e2e log-grep harness.
12	const (
13		// PickNone means no path is currently eligible (a total outage among this
14		// scheduler's paths). The Bind maps it to "no healthy path".
15		PickNone = -1
16		// PickPaced means eligible paths EXIST but every one is momentarily paced out, so
17		// this frame is shed (dropped) to bound egress and the send backlog. The paths are
18		// healthy — this is deliberate rate limiting, NOT an outage. Only a pacing-enabled
19		// weighted scheduler ever returns it.
20		PickPaced = -2
21	)
22	
23	// FrameClass tags an outbound datagram with the traffic class the pacer must treat
24	// it as (defect D22). The multipath Bind classifies each frame from the inner
25	// WireGuard message type and passes the class to Pick so a pacing scheduler can give
26	// WireGuard control frames (handshake init/response, cookie reply, keepalive) a
27	// different admission policy than bulk data. Pick() has no other frame-type
28	// visibility, so this is the ONLY channel by which frame-type reaches the pacer.
29	type FrameClass uint8
30	
31	const (
32		// ClassData is bulk/opaque data (a WireGuard transport datagram carrying tunnelled
33		// payload). It is fully paced: under a weighted scheduler with pacing enabled it is
34		// subject to the per-path token buckets and is shed (PickPaced) when they are empty.
35		ClassData FrameClass = iota
36		// ClassControl is a WireGuard control frame — handshake initiation/response, cookie
37		// reply, or keepalive. It is pacing-EXEMPT: a pacing scheduler never sheds it for an
38		// empty token bucket and never spends a data token for it, so sustained bulk overload
39		// cannot starve rekey (defect D22). Control frames originate from OUR OWN WireGuard
40		// engine on the send path (an attacker cannot inject them here), and WireGuard's own
41		// state machine bounds their rate, so exempting them from the data pace is safe.
42		ClassControl
43	)
44	
45	// Scheduler is the send-side path-selection policy the multipath Bind consults
46	// for every outbound datagram. It is the extension seam for the send scheduler:
47	// active-backup failover (ActiveBackup, the P1 MVP) is one implementation; the
48	// later weighted / FEC-aware policy (T21) is a DIFFERENT implementation of this
49	// same interface, swapped in at the composition root with no Bind change.
50	type Scheduler interface {
51		// Pick returns the index of the path the next datagram must egress on, in
52		// the path priority order the scheduler was built over (index 0 = preferred
53		// primary). It returns a negative value when no datagram should be sent this
54		// call; the negative value is one of the Pick* sentinels (PickNone for no
55		// eligible path, PickPaced for a healthy-but-paced-out shed), so the caller can
56		// tell a genuine outage apart from deliberate rate limiting. Pick is safe for
57		// concurrent callers on the Bind's send path.
58		//
59		// class is the frame's traffic class (defect D22): a pacing scheduler exempts
60		// ClassControl (WireGuard handshake/keepalive) from the data token buckets so
61		// bulk overload cannot shed control frames and starve rekey, while ClassData is
62		// fully paced. A non-pacing scheduler (active-backup) ignores class.
63		//
64		// Pick MAY be stateful: a weighted/aggregating scheduler advances its
65		// distribution bookkeeping (deficit/round-robin credits, pacing tokens, offered-
66		// load meter) on every call, so ONE Pick consumes ONE frame-selection slot.
67		// Callers that only want the liveness-derived active set refreshed — without
68		// perturbing distribution — MUST call Recompute, not Pick (see the T40 eager-
69		// failover nudge in the multipath Bind).
70		Pick(class FrameClass) int
71	
72		// Recompute refreshes the scheduler's liveness-derived selection state (which
73		// paths are eligible, which is the active primary) from the current PathHealth,
74		// and logs any active-path transition, WITHOUT selecting or consuming a frame
75		// slot: it advances no distribution/pacing/load state. It is the split-out
76		// "recompute the eligible/active set from liveness" half of the old Pick, so the
77		// eager-failover nudge (defect D18/T40) can drive an egress-lull failover recompute
78		// without stealing a weighted-distribution slot the way a spurious Pick would. For
79		// an idempotent scheduler (active-backup) it is exactly Pick without the return;
80		// for a stateful one (weighted) it is strictly the non-consuming subset. It is
81		// safe for concurrent callers and never calls back into the Bind.
82		Recompute()
83	}
84	
85	// PathQuality is the per-path measured-quality source the weighted scheduler reads
86	// to derive distribution weights: the read side of the T13 telemetry Estimate
87	// (RTT/Jitter/Loss), ANALOGOUS to PathHealth but exposing the quality snapshot

98	// DynamicScheduler is a Scheduler whose path set can change at runtime (T30): a
99	// path may be admitted or dropped WITHOUT rebuilding the scheduler or disturbing
100	// the active selection of the surviving paths. The multipath Bind holds its
101	// scheduler as a plain Scheduler and type-asserts to this richer interface only
102	// when a runtime path add/remove is requested, so the Scheduler seam (and the
103	// T21 weighted policy that will implement it) is preserved: a scheduler that
104	// does not support dynamic membership simply cannot back a reloadable bind.
105	//
106	// The index space is the SAME priority-ordered index Pick returns, and the Bind
107	// keeps its own path slice index-aligned with this scheduler's path list, so an
108	// index returned by Pick always addresses the matching path. Both methods are
109	// safe for concurrent callers.
110	type DynamicScheduler interface {
111		Scheduler
112		// AddPath admits h as a NEW lowest-priority path (highest index) and returns
113		// that index. Appending at the tail cannot change any existing path's index
114		// nor steal the active selection from a higher-priority survivor: an
115		// active-backup policy only ever selects the new path once every
116		// higher-priority path is down. h must be non-nil.
117		AddPath(h PathHealth) (int, error)
118		// RemovePath drops the path at index i (shifting higher indices down by one)
119		// and repairs the cached selection so a surviving path keeps being selected
120		// and, if the dropped path was the active one, egress fails over to the best
121		// remaining path on the next Pick. i must be in range.
122		RemovePath(i int) error
123		// SetPaths replaces the ENTIRE path/health list with health (priority order,
124		// index 0 = preferred primary) and clears any cached selection, so the next
125		// Pick re-derives the active path from live liveness. The Bind calls it on
126		// every Open to re-align the scheduler's membership with the path slice it
127		// rebuilds from the current definitions — the single reconciliation point that
128		// makes a runtime path add/remove survive a Close→Open cycle index-aligned
129		// (T30). It is safe for concurrent callers; health must be non-empty and hold
130		// no nil element.
131		SetPaths(health []PathHealth) error
132	}
133	
134	// PathHealth is the per-path liveness the scheduler consumes: the read side of
135	// the T13 telemetry liveness state machine, so the scheduler reads exactly the

138	//
139	// Concurrency contract: Pick runs on the Bind send path CONCURRENTLY with the
140	// probe/timer goroutines that mutate liveness, so a production PathHealth MUST

146	// *Prober or an explicitly-locked adapter instead. (The sched unit tests drive
147	// a raw *Liveness on a single goroutine with no concurrent writer, which is not
148	// a race and is fine for testing.)
149	type PathHealth interface {
150		State() telemetry.PathState
151	}
152

154	// composition-root placeholder used until the probe-transport wiring (a

159	type AlwaysUp struct{}
